narrative: I'm sorry to share that your law school application was rejected because our predictive model estimated a 54.9% chance that you would fail the bar exam on your first try, which is just over the threshold we use to approve admissions. This prediction is based on your academic record, including your Year 1 school decile of 4, which is below the typical student's 5.71, your Year 3 decile of 6, slightly above the average of 5.53, your LSAT score of 32.5 compared to the usual 36.7, and your undergraduate GPA of 2.9 versus the average 3.21. Your upper-middle family income bracket aligns well with many applicants, you're a full-time student like most, and as a white female, your demographics are common in the applicant pool. When the model evaluated your profile, it found your Year 3 decile to be the most influential factor, pushing the prediction toward a higher risk of bar failure despite it being decent. Next in importance was your Year 1 decile, which also nudged the odds toward failure more than other elements. Your LSAT score ranked third, contributing significantly in the same direction because it fell short of what's typically needed for strong bar performance. Your family income followed closely, then your racial background, with your undergraduate GPA also playing a supporting role in elevating the failure risk. In contrast, your gender as female was one of the few factors that gently pulled the prediction toward success, while your full-time status had minimal impact. Overall, your early academic deciles and test scores stood out as bigger drags compared to typical students who pass the bar, even though your later grades improved a bit. To boost your future chances, focus on strengthening areas you can control, like retaking the LSAT to aim closer to or above the 36.7 average, or building study habits that mirror top performers' paths from your Year 1 to Year 3 progress. Gaining more rigorous academic experience or bar prep targeted at your GPA profile could also help demonstrate readiness. With targeted improvements, you could shift this prediction in your favor for the next application cycle. Keep pushing forward—you've already shown growth in your grades, and that's a great foundation.
